messages.content: correct this run on sentence "Jojie inherited a large sum of money from her brother she can buy a new car now."
Jojie inherited a large sum of money from her brother; she can now buy a new car.
correct this run on sentence “Ash and Charles worked very hard on their project therefore they felt they deserved a high grade.”
Ash and Charles worked very hard on their project; therefore, they felt they deserved a high grade.
correct this run on sentence “I can never beat my little brother at video games, he has them all mastered.”
I can never beat my little brother at video games because he has them all mastered.
correct this run on sentence “Ace is flying to Orlando for spring break, he will be gone a week."
Ace is flying to Orlando for spring break and will be gone for a week.
correct this run on sentence “We are moving to Cincinnati in June I'll go to school there."
We are moving to Cincinnati in June, and I'll go to school there.
correct this run on sentence “The hurricane passed quickly through the area last night, and it caused a lot of damage."

The sentence "The hurricane passed quickly through the area last night, and it caused a lot of damage" is not a run-on sentence. It is a correctly punctuated compound sentence with two independent clauses joined by a conjunction "and." If you still want the sentence to be rephrased, it can be rewritten as:

"The hurricane swept through the area quickly last night, causing a lot of damage."
correct this run on sentence “We will be on vacation next week we will be unable to attend your party."
We will be on vacation next week, so we will be unable to attend your party.
correct this run on sentence “We bought shoes, luggage, hats, and swimsuits at the mall fortunately, we have a large trunk in our car."
We bought shoes, luggage, hats, and swimsuits at the mall; fortunately, we have a large trunk in our car.
correct this run on sentence “We bought shoes, luggage, hats, and swimsuits at the mall fortunately, we have a large trunk in our car."
We bought shoes, luggage, hats, and swimsuits at the mall; fortunately, we have a large trunk in our car.
correct this run on sentence “I didn't think it would be necessary to take my umbrella; after all, the sun was shining when I left home."

The sentence "I didn't think it would be necessary to take my umbrella; after all, the sun was shining when I left home." is not a run-on sentence. It is properly punctuated with a semicolon to connect two related independent clauses. However, if you would like it simplified or restructured, it could be rephrased as:

"I didn't think it would be necessary to take my umbrella because the sun was shining when I left home."
correct this run on sentence “Most of my study time is spent on trigonometry it's my toughest class."
Most of my study time is spent on trigonometry, as it's my toughest class.
correct this run on sentence “My car ran out of gas therefore I was late for my appointment."
My car ran out of gas, therefore I was late for my appointment.
correct this run on sentence “A fifth-grade student from our school won the spelling competition; she spelled words I had never heard before."

The sentence "A fifth-grade student from our school won the spelling competition; she spelled words I had never heard before." is already grammatically correct; it is not a run-on sentence. The semicolon is used appropriately to join two related independent clauses. However, if you'd like to rephrase it for stylistic reasons, you might consider:

"A fifth-grade student from our school won the spelling competition, spelling words I had never heard before."

or

"A fifth-grade student from our school won the spelling competition, and she spelled words I had never heard before."
correct this run on sentence “My younger sister collects aluminum cans to recycle and is saving the money for a new computer."

The sentence "My younger sister collects aluminum cans to recycle and is saving the money for a new computer." is not a run-on; it's a compound predicate, meaning it has two actions related to the same subject. It's correctly constructed and doesn't require correction. However, if you would like to simplify it or break it into two sentences, you might say:

"My younger sister collects aluminum cans to recycle. She is saving the money for a new computer."
correct this run on sentence “Jason's dad is a pilot for a large airline sometimes he is gone for several days at a time."
Jason's dad is a pilot for a large airline, so sometimes he is gone for several days at a time.
correct this run on sentence “My family reunion is in three months I wonder if everyone will be there."
My family reunion is in three months, and I wonder if everyone will be there.
correct this run on sentence “I've missed several classes because I was ill I hope I will pass Philosophy class."
I've missed several classes because I was ill, and I hope I will pass my Philosophy class.
correct this run on sentence “Jian is planning on going to Las Vegas for vacation; he'll need to take a lot of money."

The sentence "Jian is planning on going to Las Vegas for vacation; he'll need to take a lot of money." is actually correctly punctuated with a semicolon to connect two related independent clauses. However, you could also rephrase it as:

"Jian is planning on going to Las Vegas for vacation, and he'll need to take a lot of money."
